text.title: LB Session Persistence Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules for session-persistence design. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: If the application can be modified, session state MUST be externalized to Redis / Memcached / DB before any sticky-session config is added. Sticky sessions are a last resort, not a first choice.

rationale.source: Cloudflare LB design guide + Cache-aside / session-store patterns

rule.rationale: Stickiness pins capacity, complicates scaling, and breaks during deploys. External session storage solves the underlying problem with O(1) lookups and lets the LB stay stateless.

rule.statement: When stickiness is required, cookie-based persistence (LB-inserted or app-managed) MUST be the first choice. IP-hash is allowed ONLY when the fleet is static (no autoscaling) AND clients are NOT behind NAT or VPN.
rule.rationale: Cookies identify the real user regardless of NAT or scale events. IP-hash collapses thousands of NAT users to one backend and breaks every time the fleet scales.

rule.statement: IP-hash MUST NOT be configured in environments with autoscaling enabled OR with clients behind NAT / VPN.
rule.rationale: Adding/removing backends recomputes the hash and re-routes all sessions; corporate NAT collapses many users to one IP, overloading one backend.

rule.statement: SSL-session-ID-based persistence MAY be considered ONLY when cookies and IP-hash are unsuitable (e.g., pure TCP/TLS, no HTTP). It MUST NOT be the default — TLS 1.3 + session resumption tickets weaken its stickiness.

rationale.source: TLS 1.3 RFC 8446 + LB SSL ID references

rule.rationale: TLS 1.3 prefers tickets over session IDs; modern clients may rotate IDs per resumption. Cookie persistence is more reliable for HTTP.

rule.statement: Sticky-cookie / sticky-table entries MUST have a bounded TTL (e.g., 1 h) and MUST be invalidated on backend removal (`cookie SRVID insert indirect nocache` in HAProxy, or `sticky cookie ... expires=1h` in Nginx Plus).
rule.rationale: Unbounded stickiness pins users to a backend forever — even after rolling deploys remove that backend. TTL + invalidate-on-removal restores predictable distribution.
